license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.k6sczee
title: Quantitative Western Blot Analysis with Replicate Samples
author: LI-COR

steps_raw.instruction: Prepare an analysis spreadsheet with the normalized band intensity values for each replicate (as described in Guidelines). Western blot data must be normalized BEFORE replicate analysis is performed. See Guidelines for more information.
Using the normalized values, calculate the fold change (ratio of the experimental sample to the control) for each sample on replicate blot A (depicted in Figure 2). a a
Repeat the fold change calculations with the normalized values from replicate blots B and C (depicted in Figure 3). a

Using the fold change values from each replicate blot, calculate the mean fold change, overall % change, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation (CV) for all replicates (Table 2) (Steps 5-7).
Calculate the mean fold change of each replicate measurement. a
Calculate the standard deviatio n of the fold change for each replicate measurement.
Calculate the coefficient of variation (CV) of the fold change for each replicate measurement. a

Plot the fold change in protein expression as a function of the treatment (Figure 4). a Some journals now prefer that small datasets be presented in scatterplot format, rather than the more typical bar graphs (9 - 10). • Scatterplots clearly show the spread and distribution of your data points, making it easier for peer reviewers and readers to fully understand your results. • Bar graphs are more suitable for large datasets. Many journals now discourage the use of error bars that indicate the standard error of measurement (SEM). Error bars that indicate the standard deviation of your measurements may be more appropriate (11, 12, 13). • Figure legends should clearly state what the error bars on the graph represent, and the value of n for the experiment. Sufficient detail should be provided to indicate if replicates are technical or biological.
